questions.question: Define the following ER diagram notations with appropriate symbols: (a) Entity and Entity Set, (b) Simple, Composite, and Derived attributes, (c) Weak Entity Set, (d) Relationship and Relationship Set, (e) Multivalued attribute.
keyPoints: Entity: Real-world object represented by rectangle
Attribute: Property of entity shown as oval (simple=single oval, composite=multiple ovals, derived=dashed oval, multivalued=double oval)
Weak Entity: Double rectangle, depends on identifying owner entity
Relationship: Diamond shape connecting related entities

questions.question: Explain cardinality ratios and participation constraints in ER diagrams. For each relationship type (1:1, 1:N, M:N), provide a real-world example and explain the difference between total and partial participation with examples.
keyPoints: Cardinality: 1:1 (one-to-one), 1:N (one-to-many), M:N (many-to-many) - indicates number of entities in relationship
Participation: Total (double line) - every entity participates, Partial (single line) - participation optional
Example 1:1: Department has one Head (Employee) - each department has one head, each employee heads one department
Example 1:N: Department has many Employees - one department employs many employees, each employee belongs to one department

questions.question: Draw an ER diagram for a Hospital Management System with the following specifications: Entities: Patient (PatientID, Name, Address, DOB, Phone), Doctor (DoctorID, Name, Specialization, Salary), Ward (WardNo, Capacity, Type), Medicine (MedicineID, Name, Cost). Relationships: (a) Doctor treats Patient with attribute 'TreatmentDate', (b) Patient occupies Ward with attribute 'AdmissionDate', (c) Doctor prescribes Medicine to Patient. Specify cardinalities (1:1, 1:N, M:N) and participation constraints (total/partial) for each relationship. Identify primary keys and indicate weak entities if any.
keyPoints: Patient and Doctor are strong entities with PatientID and DoctorID as primary keys
Relationships: Doctor-treats-Patient (1:N or M:N based on assumption), Patient-occupies-Ward (1:N), Doctor-prescribes-Medicine-to-Patient (M:N)
Ward can be weak entity if WardNo is not unique across hospital, Patient-Ward relationship shows total participation if every patient must occupy a ward
Derived attribute: Can calculate Age from DOB; Composite attribute: Address can have Street, City, Pincode

questions.question: Consider an Online Shopping System. Design a complete ER diagram showing: Entities: Customer (CustomerID, Name, Email, Password, Address), Product (ProductID, Name, Description, Price, StockQuantity), Order (OrderID, OrderDate, TotalAmount, Status), Seller (SellerID, Name, Rating). Relationships: (a) Customer places Order, (b) Order contains multiple Products (with quantity), (c) Seller supplies Products, (d) Customer reviews Products (with Rating and Comment). For each relationship, determine: (i) Cardinality ratio, (ii) Participation constraint (total/partial), (iii) Attributes if any. Also identify and represent: composite attributes, multivalued attributes, derived attributes, and primary keys for all entities.
keyPoints: Customer-Order: 1:N cardinality (one customer places many orders), Customer has total participation if every order must have a customer
Order-Product (contains): M:N relationship with 'Quantity' as relationship attribute, creates associative entity OrderItem
Seller-Product: 1:N or M:N (seller can supply many products, product may have multiple sellers), partial participation for both
Customer-Product (reviews): M:N with 'Rating' and 'Comment' as attributes; Address is composite (Street, City, Pincode); StockQuantity can derive if tracked separately
